text.content: Multiple Features Fusion Attention Mechanism Enhanced Deep Knowledge Tracing for Student Performance Prediction

title.plain_text: Multiple Features Fusion Attention Mechanism Enhanced Deep Knowledge Tracing for Student Performance Prediction

tables.heading: Summary Table KT
rows: Correct on next exercise on first attempt

Previous models primarily relied on limited exercise features, ignored most student behavior features, and used manual feature selection with uniform feature weighting. Existing models also struggled with processing long learning sequences and capturing the varying importance of different features.

The MFA-DKT framework addresses these limitations by automatically processing both behavior and exercise features using Principal Component Analysis. It employs an LSTM network with an attention mechanism to dynamically weight feature importance.
PCA for feature dimensionality reduction, Removal of NaNs, duplicates, and text features
{AUC: 0.98 - MFA-DKT, Accuracy: 0.97 - MFA-DKT, RMSE - 0.26 - MFA-DKT, MAE-0.20 - MFA-DKT}

MFA-DKT outperforms all baselines, better accuracy with larger training set.

Second experiment compares model against different combinations of features and outperforms all others.
Ignores relationship between knowledge concepts, uses PCA for feature embeddings

blocks.text: Greater ability to produce content has contributed towards an increase of supply in educational contents. Tracking performance in these contexts is especially important, as it opens the door of personalized support to students in need.

blocks.text: There is a distinction between exercise features - related with knowledge concepts and how they lead to a student’s response - and behaviour features - how a student behaves and/or what is his/her background. Combining multiple types of features is, generally speaking, a better approach than working with “isolated features”. However, combining data from multiple sources is relatively hard. Over the years, the prediction of performance has seen advancements using Knowledge Tracing, Deep Learning. Deep Knowledge Tracing, more specifically, combines both DL and KT as it intends to use the Sequential Nature of RNNs into a knowledge training task (KT - does a student know a concept or not).

blocks.text: So, essencially, the research gap is that the models that predict student performance either focus on Knowledge Tracing - exercise features - OR behavior related features, with little work being done to combine BOTH types of Features. So, the authors suggest a model that fully utilizes both exercise and behaviour features - MFA-DKT.

blocks.text: Cognitive Diagnosis: This approach tries to infer knowledge-states from exercise records. Item Response Theory is a popular cognitive model used in this sort of applications. This model is meant to “infer knowledge states” and use these inferred knowledge states (also to be understood as a latent variable for mastery of ideas/concepts) to predict student performance.

blocks.text: Knowledge Tracing: Knowledge Tracing works in the following manner: you are trying to predict whether a student will get the next answer correctly or not. It also uses exercise records to compute 4 parameters - these parameters (under BKT) will then be used to compute the probability of getting the next answer right.

blocks.text: Attention models draw from intuition: when presented with a lot of information, we tend not to focus on thon a certain segment. Think of it like a vector of weights that assign different importances to the elements of a sequence.

blocks.text: Results

text.content: Results

rich_text.plain_text: Results

blocks.text: Repeated Holdout method of sequences. When comparing the results of the MFA DKT vs the Baseline approaches such as BKT, the authors found that the traditional methods were not as effective for the proposed task. In fact, MFA DKT outperformed all other approaches across all metrics whereas BKT was consistently the worse model. Also, the performance always seems to increase the larger the training set is: as it consistently shows that 90% training size for the Repeated Holdout achieves the best scores.

blocks.text: At a second level of experiment, the authors look to analyze what is the impact of including the behaviour related features into the models used. For comparison purposes, the authors look into benchmarking MFA DKT agains DKT (which uses no features) and IRF DKT (which uses a few student features).  Again, MFA DKT which uses attention mechanisms to extract “information” about all student features seems to outperform all other models used.

blocks.text: Implications

text.content: Implications

rich_text.plain_text: Implications

blocks.text: Authors argue that a greater ability to identify knowledge states in students opens the door for better learning pathways that are directed towards the students “actual needs”. This could allow, for example, instructors to spend more time covering parts where students struggle more than the parts where the students seem to struggle less. As a limitation, the authors discuss that the work “ignores” the relationship between knowledge concepts when predicting student performance: meaning that prior knowledge on topic does not seem to still be fully realized on this model.

text.content: Authors argue that a greater ability to identify knowledge states in students opens the door for better learning pathways that are directed towards the students “actual needs”. blocks.text: Final Overview: The paper introduces a novel way of performing knowledge tracing. The novelty aspect resides into the full incorporation of not only exercise related features, but behaviour related features as well. They for some reason use PCA to create embeddings of the features. This model seems to outperform other approaches to knowledge tracing on the ASSISTments dataset.
